topic: Two siblings inherit their grandmother's house
raw_output:
[story 1]

The solicitor’s crisp papers felt like sandpaper against my palms. I stared at the deed, the ink blurring under the harsh afternoon light. My brother, Mark, was already practically vibrating with excitement, practically bouncing on the balls of his feet, already picturing dusty antique furniture and endless summer afternoons. I just stood there, arms crossed so tightly my knuckles were white, refusing to even glance at the property lines. "This is ridiculous," I muttered, the sound tight and sharp in the quiet drawing-room. "She left this entire estate to him. How could she possibly think this was fair?" I took a deep, shaky breath, trying to steady the sudden tremor in my chest. Every detail of the house—the peeling wallpaper, the creaking floorboards—felt like a personal slight, a testament to some unfair distribution of affection.

[story 2]

"Honestly, Mark, you’re going to ruin everything with your enthusiasm," I said, my voice clipped and low, refusing to meet his eager gaze as he gestured wildly toward the sprawling, slightly dilapidated manor. I paced the length of the foyer three times, the worn Persian rug muffling the sound of my heavy footsteps. The sheer audacity of it—my grandmother, who always favored my quieter, more scholarly pursuits, handing this sprawling, messy burden over to the one who always chased the next thrill—it chafed deep within me. I stopped abruptly by the grand staircase, gripping the banister until my fingers ached. "It's not just an inheritance; it’s an imposition," I stated, my jaw tight. "I don't see how this responsibility falls to me."

[story 3]

I leaned against the mantelpiece, crossing my arms over my chest, letting the silence stretch until it became almost unbearable. The air in the library smelled of old leather and forgotten lavender, a scent that only amplified my simmering displeasure. Mark was already halfway through a breathless inventory, practically giddy about the potential for renovation. I watched him, a cold, hard expression settling over my features. "You know," I began, my tone dripping with forced politeness, "I suppose I should be grateful. But this house... it feels like a punishment." I pushed off the mantelpiece, taking a deliberate step away from the warmth of the fireplace. "She never intended for me to deal with this kind of chaos. It’s simply not right."
